answers:
**1. What base branch should this PR target, and why?**
`main`. It's the only integration/trunk branch in the repository — `git branch -a -vv` (both in the working copy and in the bare `origin`) shows exactly three branches total: `main`, `feat/weighted-scorer`, and `feat/pairwise-metric`; there is no `develop` or release branch. `feat/weighted-scorer` is not a viable target: as shown in Q2, its content is already on `main`, so targeting it would aim the PR at a branch that is itself stale/superseded.

**2. Where was `feat/pairwise-metric` created from, and what is that starting point's status relative to `main`?**
It was created from **`feat/weighted-scorer`**, not from `main`. `feat/pairwise-metric`'s first unique commit (`7b2e100`, "X1: pairwise-comparison metric") has `feat/weighted-scorer`'s tip (`ac9d505`, "W3: token-overlap helper in metrics") as its parent, and `git merge-base feat/weighted-scorer feat/pairwise-metric` returns `ac9d505` exactly — confirming full linear ancestry (all of `feat/weighted-scorer`'s commits `d6fdbc3`/`2d3029b`/`ac9d505` are baked into `feat/pairwise-metric`'s history).

`feat/weighted-scorer`'s commits are **not** an ancestor of `main` (`git merge-base --is-ancestor feat/weighted-scorer main` → false), but its content is already on `main`: `main`'s tip `1971f08` ("Refactor scorer for weighted metrics (#42)", 2026-06-13) has an aggregate diff from the common base (`65204ad`) whose `git patch-id` is byte-identical to the aggregate diff of `feat/weighted-scorer`'s three commits from the same base — same two files, same +16/-3 line counts, one day after `feat/pairwise-metric`'s commits were made. This is conclusive evidence that `feat/weighted-scorer` was **squash-merged into `main`** as PR #42, under a new commit hash that git's ancestry graph has no way of linking back to the original branch.

What this means for how to proceed: this is not a "blocked, still in progress" dependency — it's a **completed dependency recorded under different commits**. I cannot rely on `main` "already containing" the dependency from git's point of view; a naive diff/PR against `main` will re-present all of `feat/weighted-scorer`'s commits as new, unreviewed work (see Q3), and it will actually conflict, not just look redundant. The branch must be rebased to drop the now-redundant commits before opening the PR (Q4).

**3. What does the diff against `main` actually contain, and is all of it pairwise-metric work?**
`git diff main...feat/pairwise-metric --stat` (three-dot, i.e. against the merge-base `65204ad` — what a GitHub PR view would show): **3 files changed, 21 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)** — `prompts/grader.txt` +1, `scoring/metrics.py` +10, `scoring/scorer.py` +13/-3, across 5 commits (`git log --oneline main..feat/pairwise-metric`: W1, W2, W3, X1, X2).

**No, not all of it is pairwise-metric work.** Isolating the branch's own contribution via `git diff feat/weighted-scorer..feat/pairwise-metric --stat` shows the genuine pairwise feature is only **2 files, 5 insertions, 0 deletions**: the new `pairwise_preference()` function in `scoring/metrics.py` (4 lines) and one line added to `prompts/grader.txt` ("For pairwise comparisons, reply with A or B."). The remaining 16 insertions and all 3 deletions — every change in `scoring/scorer.py`, plus 6 of the 10 insertions in `scoring/metrics.py` (the `overlap()` helper) — are `feat/weighted-scorer`'s content, present only because `feat/pairwise-metric` branched from `feat/weighted-scorer` and was never rebased after that work landed on `main` as #42. This is a benign, explainable artifact of branch ancestry, not scope creep by the author.

It's also not merely cosmetic duplication: a read-only `git merge-tree main feat/pairwise-metric` dry-run produced an actual **CONFLICT (content) in `scoring/metrics.py`** (exit code 1). Inspecting the three conflict blobs directly (`git cat-file -p` on the base/ours/theirs SHAs) showed why: `main` and `feat/pairwise-metric` each independently added an identical `overlap()` function at the end of the file, and `feat/pairwise-metric` additionally appends `pairwise_preference()` right after it — git's 3-way merge can't reconcile that automatically. By contrast, `scoring/scorer.py` is byte-identical between the two branches already (`git diff main feat/pairwise-metric -- scoring/scorer.py` is empty — no real difference, just redundant history), and `prompts/grader.txt` is a clean one-sided addition with no overlap. So of the 3 files touched, only 1 (`metrics.py`) has any real conflict, but the branch as a whole still carries far more history than its actual feature warrants.

**4. What must be done, in what order, before opening the PR?**
1. Confirm the branch is current against `origin` (`git rev-parse main origin/main` — both already resolve to `1971f08...` here, so `main` is up to date; in a live repo, `git fetch origin` first).
2. **Rebase `feat/pairwise-metric` to drop the now-redundant `feat/weighted-scorer` commits**, replaying only the genuine pairwise work onto current `main`:
   `git rebase --onto main feat/weighted-scorer feat/pairwise-metric`
   This replays exactly the commits reachable from `feat/pairwise-metric` but not from `feat/weighted-scorer` (X1 and X2) onto `main`. I validated this converges cleanly (no conflicts) by simulating the equivalent 3-way merge with `feat/weighted-scorer` as the explicit merge base (`git merge-tree feat/weighted-scorer main feat/pairwise-metric`), which returned exit code 0 for both touched files and produced output identical to the isolated 5-line pairwise diff from Q3.
3. **Verify the result**: re-run `git diff main...feat/pairwise-metric --stat` (should now show only 2 files / 5 insertions / 0 deletions) and `git log --oneline main..feat/pairwise-metric` (should show only X1, X2); run the test suite (`tests/test_scoring.py`) — note it currently only exercises `exactness` and never calls `overlap()` or the new `pairwise_preference()`, so a green test run would not by itself prove the new function is correct. Also worth flagging: `git grep` found `pairwise_preference()` defined but never called anywhere (not in `scoring/scorer.py`, not in `jobs/run_batch.py`) — confirm with the author whether that's intentional (a prompt-support primitive landing ahead of its caller) so reviewers aren't left asking where it's used.
4. **Force-push the rewritten branch**: rebasing rewrites the X1/X2 commit hashes that are already pushed as `origin/feat/pairwise-metric`, so a plain push will be rejected. Use `git push --force-with-lease origin feat/pairwise-metric` — and confirm with the author first, since this rewrites already-shared history (I did not do this; it's a mutating, hard-to-reverse operation outside my read-only scope).
5. **Open the PR**: `feat/pairwise-metric` → `main`. At this point the diff will contain only the pairwise-comparison work, with no leftover `feat/weighted-scorer` content and no conflicts.
6. Optional, non-blocking cleanup to mention to the author: `feat/weighted-scorer` is now fully superseded by `main` (#42) and could be deleted (locally and on `origin`) to prevent this same confusion recurring — a call for whoever owns that branch, not something to fold into this PR unasked.
report:
## Plan

The task was to prepare `feat/pairwise-metric` for a PR without making any changes: locate the branch, determine what base branch it should target, trace its true point of origin and that origin's current relationship to `main`, determine exactly what a diff against `main` would contain and whether it's all in-scope, and lay out the cleanup sequence needed before the PR would review cleanly. Everything below was done with read-only git plumbing/porcelain commands (`status`, `log`, `diff`, `merge-base`, `cherry`, `patch-id`, `cat-file`, and the non-mutating `git merge-tree` dry-run merge simulation) plus file reads — nothing was committed, checked out, merged, rebased, or pushed.

## Actions

1. **Skill: `superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch`** — consulted for its base-branch-determination approach (`git merge-base HEAD main`). Its later steps (present merge/PR/discard options and execute the choice) directly conflict with the task's strict read-only mandate, so I used only its diagnostic framing and did not follow its action/execution steps.
2. `pwd && ls -la && git rev-parse --show-toplevel && git status` (workspace root) — the workspace root itself is **not** a git repo; it contains 14 sibling project directories plus `README.md`.
3. `Read README.md` — documents each directory as an independent git repo; flags `answer-scoring-pipeline/` as "LLM answer-scoring pipeline (**PR in flight**)".
4. `cd answer-scoring-pipeline && git status && git branch -a -vv && git remote -v` — HEAD is on `feat/pairwise-metric` (`1bfecb6`), clean working tree; sibling branches `feat/weighted-scorer` (`ac9d505`) and `main` (`1971f08`); `origin` is a local bare repo at `.../fixtures/pipeline/answer-scoring-pipeline-origin.git`, mirroring all three branches.
5. `git log --oneline --graph --decorate --all` + `git merge-base main feat/pairwise-metric` + `git merge-base main feat/weighted-scorer` + `git merge-base feat/weighted-scorer feat/pairwise-metric` — mapped topology: common ancestor `65204ad` ("base scoring pipeline"); `feat/weighted-scorer` = `65204ad` + 3 commits; `feat/pairwise-metric` = `feat/weighted-scorer`'s tip + 2 more commits; `main` advanced independently from `65204ad` via a single commit `1971f08`.
6. `git show --stat 1971f08`, `git log -1 --format=... 1971f08`, `git log --format=... -3 feat/weighted-scorer` — `1971f08` = "Refactor scorer for weighted metrics (#42)", author "Fixture Bot", dated 2026-06-13, touching `scoring/metrics.py` (+6) and `scoring/scorer.py` (+13/-3) — same two files, same line counts as `feat/weighted-scorer`'s three commits (dated 2026-06-10/11).
7. `git diff 65204ad..feat/weighted-scorer --stat` vs `git diff 65204ad..main --stat`, then `git diff 65204ad..feat/weighted-scorer | git patch-id --stable` vs `git diff 65204ad..main | git patch-id --stable` — **identical patch-id** (`4b94b656...`) on both sides.
8. `git diff main...feat/pairwise-metric --stat`, `git log --oneline main..feat/pairwise-metric`, `git merge-base --is-ancestor feat/weighted-scorer main` (exit 1 → not an ancestor), `git cherry main feat/pairwise-metric` (all 5 commits reported `+`, i.e. not found on `main` — per-commit cherry-detection misses the squash).
9. `git diff feat/weighted-scorer..feat/pairwise-metric --stat` and full diff — isolated the *true* pairwise-only contribution.
10. `git --version` (2.52.0), then `git merge-tree main feat/pairwise-metric` — read-only merge simulation; exit code 1, **real conflict** reported in `scoring/metrics.py`. Re-ran `git status` / `git branch -vv` immediately after to confirm zero side effects.
11. `git cat-file -p` on the three conflict blob SHAs (base/ours/theirs for `scoring/metrics.py`) — read the exact conflicting content.
12. `git diff main feat/pairwise-metric -- scoring/scorer.py` (empty → byte-identical) and `-- prompts/grader.txt` (clean one-sided addition).
13. `git log --all --format=... | sort`, `git stash list` (empty), `git ls-tree -r main`, `git rev-parse main origin/main` (both `1971f08...`, in sync), `git --git-dir=<bare origin> branch -vv` (same 3 branches on origin), search for `CONTRIBUTING`/`PULL_REQUEST`/`.github` (none found).
14. `git diff main feat/pairwise-metric -- tests/test_scoring.py` (empty), `git show feat/pairwise-metric:tests/test_scoring.py`, `git show feat/pairwise-metric:scoring/scorer.py` — test file untouched by either branch, only covers `exactness`.
15. `git grep -n "pairwise_preference|pairwise" feat/pairwise-metric` — the new function is defined and referenced only in the prompt text; not called anywhere else in the codebase.
16. `git rev-parse --git-dir --git-common-dir --is-bare-repository --abbrev-ref HEAD` — confirmed a normal checkout (not a worktree, not detached, not bare).
17. `git merge-tree feat/weighted-scorer main feat/pairwise-metric` (legacy 3-arg form, explicit base) — read-only simulation of what `git rebase --onto main feat/weighted-scorer feat/pairwise-metric` would converge to: exit code 0, both touched files merge cleanly, output matches the isolated pairwise-only diff from step 9 exactly. Reconfirmed no side effects afterward.

## Answers

**1. What base branch should this PR target, and why?**
`main`. It's the only integration/trunk branch in the repository — `git branch -a -vv` (both in the working copy and in the bare `origin`) shows exactly three branches total: `main`, `feat/weighted-scorer`, and `feat/pairwise-metric`; there is no `develop` or release branch. `feat/weighted-scorer` is not a viable target: as shown in Q2, its content is already on `main`, so targeting it would aim the PR at a branch that is itself stale/superseded.

**2. Where was `feat/pairwise-metric` created from, and what is that starting point's status relative to `main`?**
It was created from **`feat/weighted-scorer`**, not from `main`. `feat/pairwise-metric`'s first unique commit (`7b2e100`, "X1: pairwise-comparison metric") has `feat/weighted-scorer`'s tip (`ac9d505`, "W3: token-overlap helper in metrics") as its parent, and `git merge-base feat/weighted-scorer feat/pairwise-metric` returns `ac9d505` exactly — confirming full linear ancestry (all of `feat/weighted-scorer`'s commits `d6fdbc3`/`2d3029b`/`ac9d505` are baked into `feat/pairwise-metric`'s history).

`feat/weighted-scorer`'s commits are **not** an ancestor of `main` (`git merge-base --is-ancestor feat/weighted-scorer main` → false), but its content is already on `main`: `main`'s tip `1971f08` ("Refactor scorer for weighted metrics (#42)", 2026-06-13) has an aggregate diff from the common base (`65204ad`) whose `git patch-id` is byte-identical to the aggregate diff of `feat/weighted-scorer`'s three commits from the same base — same two files, same +16/-3 line counts, one day after `feat/pairwise-metric`'s commits were made. This is conclusive evidence that `feat/weighted-scorer` was **squash-merged into `main`** as PR #42, under a new commit hash that git's ancestry graph has no way of linking back to the original branch.

What this means for how to proceed: this is not a "blocked, still in progress" dependency — it's a **completed dependency recorded under different commits**. I cannot rely on `main` "already containing" the dependency from git's point of view; a naive diff/PR against `main` will re-present all of `feat/weighted-scorer`'s commits as new, unreviewed work (see Q3), and it will actually conflict, not just look redundant. The branch must be rebased to drop the now-redundant commits before opening the PR (Q4).

**3. What does the diff against `main` actually contain, and is all of it pairwise-metric work?**
`git diff main...feat/pairwise-metric --stat` (three-dot, i.e. against the merge-base `65204ad` — what a GitHub PR view would show): **3 files changed, 21 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)** — `prompts/grader.txt` +1, `scoring/metrics.py` +10, `scoring/scorer.py` +13/-3, across 5 commits (`git log --oneline main..feat/pairwise-metric`: W1, W2, W3, X1, X2).

**No, not all of it is pairwise-metric work.** Isolating the branch's own contribution via `git diff feat/weighted-scorer..feat/pairwise-metric --stat` shows the genuine pairwise feature is only **2 files, 5 insertions, 0 deletions**: the new `pairwise_preference()` function in `scoring/metrics.py` (4 lines) and one line added to `prompts/grader.txt` ("For pairwise comparisons, reply with A or B."). The remaining 16 insertions and all 3 deletions — every change in `scoring/scorer.py`, plus 6 of the 10 insertions in `scoring/metrics.py` (the `overlap()` helper) — are `feat/weighted-scorer`'s content, present only because `feat/pairwise-metric` branched from `feat/weighted-scorer` and was never rebased after that work landed on `main` as #42. This is a benign, explainable artifact of branch ancestry, not scope creep by the author.

It's also not merely cosmetic duplication: a read-only `git merge-tree main feat/pairwise-metric` dry-run produced an actual **CONFLICT (content) in `scoring/metrics.py`** (exit code 1). Inspecting the three conflict blobs directly (`git cat-file -p` on the base/ours/theirs SHAs) showed why: `main` and `feat/pairwise-metric` each independently added an identical `overlap()` function at the end of the file, and `feat/pairwise-metric` additionally appends `pairwise_preference()` right after it — git's 3-way merge can't reconcile that automatically. By contrast, `scoring/scorer.py` is byte-identical between the two branches already (`git diff main feat/pairwise-metric -- scoring/scorer.py` is empty — no real difference, just redundant history), and `prompts/grader.txt` is a clean one-sided addition with no overlap. So of the 3 files touched, only 1 (`metrics.py`) has any real conflict, but the branch as a whole still carries far more history than its actual feature warrants.

**4. What must be done, in what order, before opening the PR?**
1. Confirm the branch is current against `origin` (`git rev-parse main origin/main` — both already resolve to `1971f08...` here, so `main` is up to date; in a live repo, `git fetch origin` first).
2. **Rebase `feat/pairwise-metric` to drop the now-redundant `feat/weighted-scorer` commits**, replaying only the genuine pairwise work onto current `main`:
   `git rebase --onto main feat/weighted-scorer feat/pairwise-metric`
   This replays exactly the commits reachable from `feat/pairwise-metric` but not from `feat/weighted-scorer` (X1 and X2) onto `main`. I validated this converges cleanly (no conflicts) by simulating the equivalent 3-way merge with `feat/weighted-scorer` as the explicit merge base (`git merge-tree feat/weighted-scorer main feat/pairwise-metric`), which returned exit code 0 for both touched files and produced output identical to the isolated 5-line pairwise diff from Q3.
3. **Verify the result**: re-run `git diff main...feat/pairwise-metric --stat` (should now show only 2 files / 5 insertions / 0 deletions) and `git log --oneline main..feat/pairwise-metric` (should show only X1, X2); run the test suite (`tests/test_scoring.py`) — note it currently only exercises `exactness` and never calls `overlap()` or the new `pairwise_preference()`, so a green test run would not by itself prove the new function is correct. Also worth flagging: `git grep` found `pairwise_preference()` defined but never called anywhere (not in `scoring/scorer.py`, not in `jobs/run_batch.py`) — confirm with the author whether that's intentional (a prompt-support primitive landing ahead of its caller) so reviewers aren't left asking where it's used.
4. **Force-push the rewritten branch**: rebasing rewrites the X1/X2 commit hashes that are already pushed as `origin/feat/pairwise-metric`, so a plain push will be rejected. Use `git push --force-with-lease origin feat/pairwise-metric` — and confirm with the author first, since this rewrites already-shared history (I did not do this; it's a mutating, hard-to-reverse operation outside my read-only scope).
5. **Open the PR**: `feat/pairwise-metric` → `main`. At this point the diff will contain only the pairwise-comparison work, with no leftover `feat/weighted-scorer` content and no conflicts.
6. Optional, non-blocking cleanup to mention to the author: `feat/weighted-scorer` is now fully superseded by `main` (#42) and could be deleted (locally and on `origin`) to prevent this same confusion recurring — a call for whoever owns that branch, not something to fold into this PR unasked.

## Next steps

If I were allowed to act: fetch to reconfirm `origin` state, run the validated `git rebase --onto main feat/weighted-scorer feat/pairwise-metric`, re-check the stat/log output to confirm the diff is now exactly the 2-file/5-line pairwise change, run the existing test suite, then (after explicit confirmation, since it rewrites pushed history) `git push --force-with-lease origin feat/pairwise-metric`, and finally open the PR with a description noting it was rebased past #42 and flagging that `pairwise_preference()` isn't wired into the scorer yet, so the author/reviewer can decide if that belongs in this PR or a follow-up.

## Confidence and gaps

- **High confidence**, directly observed: branch topology, all merge-base relationships, the three-dot diff contents, the isolated pairwise-only diff, and the merge conflict — all came from live git plumbing output and a real (read-only) `git merge-tree` simulation, not inference.
- **High confidence, one inferential step**: that `1971f08` on `main` *is* the squash-merge of `feat/weighted-scorer`. This rests on identical patch-id + identical per-file stats + consistent timing (one day after X1/X2), which is about as strong as circumstantial git evidence gets — but `origin` here is a local bare repo, not a hosted GitHub/GitLab instance, so I couldn't cross-check an actual "squashed and merged" PR #42 page/API to remove all doubt.
- **Medium confidence**: recommendations around PR description content and whether wiring up `pairwise_preference()` belongs in this PR — those are authorial/review judgment calls that git state alone can't settle, only surface.
- **Not checked**: CI configuration — none exists (no `.github/`, no CONTRIBUTING/PR template found), so I can't say whether an automated pipeline would independently catch the conflict before merge. I did not examine the other 13 sibling repos in the workspace; the README and branch search already confirmed `feat/pairwise-metric` exists only in `answer-scoring-pipeline`.
- I deliberately avoided running `git fetch` (even though it only touches remote-tracking refs, it's still a write) and instead confirmed sync via `git rev-parse main origin/main`, which is equally authoritative here since `origin` is a local path I could read directly — flagging this in case a real remote would warrant an actual fetch first.

This is conclusive evidence that `feat/weighted-scorer` was **squash-merged into `main`** as PR #42, under a new commit hash that git's ancestry graph has no way of linking back to the original branch.

What this means for how to proceed: this is not a "blocked, still in progress" dependency — it's a **completed dependency recorded under different commits**. I cannot rely on `main` "already containing" the dependency from git's point of view; a naive diff/PR against `main` will re-present all of `feat/weighted-scorer`'s commits as new, unreviewed work (see Q3), and it will actually conflict, not just look redundant. The branch must be rebased to drop the now-redundant commits before opening the PR (Q4).

**3. What does the diff against `main` actually contain, and is all of it pairwise-metric work?**
`git diff main...feat/pairwise-metric --stat` (three-dot, i.e. against the merge-base `65204ad` — what a GitHub PR view would show): **3 files changed, 21 insertions(+), 3 deletions(-)** — `prompts/grader.txt` +1, `scoring/metrics.py` +10, `scoring/scorer.py` +13/-3, across 5 commits (`git log --oneline main..feat/pairwise-metric`: W1, W2, W3, X1, X2).

**No, not all of it is pairwise-metric work.** Isolating the branch's own contribution via `git diff feat/weighted-scorer..feat/pairwise-metric --stat` shows the genuine pairwise feature is only **2 files, 5 insertions, 0 deletions**: the new `pairwise_preference()` function in `scoring/metrics.py` (4 lines) and one line added to `prompts/grader.txt` ("For pairwise comparisons, reply with A or B."). The remaining 16 insertions and all 3 deletions — every change in `scoring/scorer.py`, plus 6 of the 10 insertions in `scoring/metrics.py` (the `overlap()` helper) — are `feat/weighted-scorer`'s content, present only because `feat/pairwise-metric` branched from `feat/weighted-scorer` and was never rebased after that work landed on `main` as #42. This is a benign, explainable artifact of branch ancestry, not scope creep by the author.

It's also not merely cosmetic duplication: a read-only `git merge-tree main feat/pairwise-metric` dry-run produced an actual **CONFLICT (content) in `scoring/metrics.py`** (exit code 1). Inspecting the three conflict blobs directly (`git cat-file -p` on the base/ours/theirs SHAs) showed why: `main` and `feat/pairwise-metric` each independently added an identical `overlap()` function at the end of the file, and `feat/pairwise-metric` additionally appends `pairwise_preference()` right after it — git's 3-way merge can't reconcile that automatically. By contrast, `scoring/scorer.py` is byte-identical between the two branches already (`git diff main feat/pairwise-metric -- scoring/scorer.py` is empty — no real difference, just redundant history), and `prompts/grader.txt` is a clean one-sided addition with no overlap. So of the 3 files touched, only 1 (`metrics.py`) has any real conflict, but the branch as a whole still carries far more history than its actual feature warrants.

**4. What must be done, in what order, before opening the PR?**
1. Confirm the branch is current against `origin` (`git rev-parse main origin/main` — both already resolve to `1971f08...` here, so `main` is up to date; in a live repo, `git fetch origin` first).
2. **Rebase `feat/pairwise-metric` to drop the now-redundant `feat/weighted-scorer` commits**, replaying only the genuine pairwise work onto current `main`:
   `git rebase --onto main feat/weighted-scorer feat/pairwise-metric`
   This replays exactly the commits reachable from `feat/pairwise-metric` but not from `feat/weighted-scorer` (X1 and X2) onto `main`. I validated this converges cleanly (no conflicts) by simulating the equivalent 3-way merge with `feat/weighted-scorer` as the explicit merge base (`git merge-tree feat/weighted-scorer main feat/pairwise-metric`), which returned exit code 0 for both touched files and produced output identical to the isolated 5-line pairwise diff from Q3.
3. **Verify the result**: re-run `git diff main...feat/pairwise-metric --stat` (should now show only 2 files / 5 insertions / 0 deletions) and `git log --oneline main..feat/pairwise-metric` (should show only X1, X2); run the test suite (`tests/test_scoring.py`) — note it currently only exercises `exactness` and never calls `overlap()` or the new `pairwise_preference()`, so a green test run would not by itself prove the new function is correct. Also worth flagging: `git grep` found `pairwise_preference()` defined but never called anywhere (not in `scoring/scorer.py`, not in `jobs/run_batch.py`) — confirm with the author whether that's intentional (a prompt-support primitive landing ahead of its caller) so reviewers aren't left asking where it's used.
4. **Force-push the rewritten branch**: rebasing rewrites the X1/X2 commit hashes that are already pushed as `origin/feat/pairwise-metric`, so a plain push will be rejected. Use `git push --force-with-lease origin feat/pairwise-metric` — and confirm with the author first, since this rewrites already-shared history (I did not do this; it's a mutating, hard-to-reverse operation outside my read-only scope).
5. **Open the PR**: `feat/pairwise-metric` → `main`. At this point the diff will contain only the pairwise-comparison work, with no leftover `feat/weighted-scorer` content and no conflicts.
6. Optional, non-blocking cleanup to mention to the author: `feat/weighted-scorer` is now fully superseded by `main` (#42) and could be deleted (locally and on `origin`) to prevent this same confusion recurring — a call for whoever owns that branch, not something to fold into this PR unasked.
sections.confidence and gaps:
- **High confidence**, directly observed: branch topology, all merge-base relationships, the three-dot diff contents, the isolated pairwise-only diff, and the merge conflict — all came from live git plumbing output and a real (read-only) `git merge-tree` simulation, not inference.
- **High confidence, one inferential step**: that `1971f08` on `main` *is* the squash-merge of `feat/weighted-scorer`. This rests on identical patch-id + identical per-file stats + consistent timing (one day after X1/X2), which is about as strong as circumstantial git evidence gets — but `origin` here is a local bare repo, not a hosted GitHub/GitLab instance, so I couldn't cross-check an actual "squashed and merged" PR #42 page/API to remove all doubt.
- **Medium confidence**: recommendations around PR description content and whether wiring up `pairwise_preference()` belongs in this PR — those are authorial/review judgment calls that git state alone can't settle, only surface.
- **Not checked**: CI configuration — none exists (no `.github/`, no CONTRIBUTING/PR template found), so I can't say whether an automated pipeline would independently catch the conflict before merge. I did not examine the other 13 sibling repos in the workspace; the README and branch search already confirmed `feat/pairwise-metric` exists only in `answer-scoring-pipeline`.
- I deliberately avoided running `git fetch` (even though it only touches remote-tracking refs, it's still a write) and instead confirmed sync via `git rev-parse main origin/main`, which is equally authoritative here since `origin` is a local path I could read directly — flagging this in case a real remote would warrant an actual fetch first.
sections.next steps: If I were allowed to act: fetch to reconfirm `origin` state, run the validated `git rebase --onto main feat/weighted-scorer feat/pairwise-metric`, re-check the stat/log output to confirm the diff is now exactly the 2-file/5-line pairwise change, run the existing test suite, then (after explicit confirmation, since it rewrites pushed history) `git push --force-with-lease origin feat/pairwise-metric`, and finally open the PR with a description noting it was rebased past #42 and flagging that `pairwise_preference()` isn't wired into the scorer yet, so the author/reviewer can decide if that belongs in this PR or a follow-up.
sections.plan: The task was to prepare `feat/pairwise-metric` for a PR without making any changes: locate the branch, determine what base branch it should target, trace its true point of origin and that origin's current relationship to `main`, determine exactly what a diff against `main` would contain and whether it's all in-scope, and lay out the cleanup sequence needed before the PR would review cleanly. Everything below was done with read-only git plumbing/porcelain commands (`status`, `log`, `diff`, `merge-base`, `cherry`, `patch-id`, `cat-file`, and the non-mutating `git merge-tree` dry-run merge simulation) plus file reads — nothing was committed, checked out, merged, rebased, or pushed.
